quote: People do that all the time, actually, David. You would be quite surprised. Why? Because during your off peak hours, like, when the electricity isn't so high, the battery pulls from it. And then during on peak hours, the battery then is in use, so then you're not spending money during the high electricity rates.
stewart_read: David called the clarifying question 'stupid' and the rep responded by defending it with a 35-second technical explanation of grid-charging arbitrage — closer-tier content. The reactive read is likely correct: the rep felt challenged and over-explained to justify the question. However, the explanation DID produce a genuine curiosity response at 01:45 ('I've never heard of such a thing'), which is a partial win the rep stumbled into rather than engineered. The coaching implication is about efficiency, not elimination — the explanation worked, but it was the long way around.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep deliberately used the grid-charging explanation to reframe David's skepticism and create curiosity before moving into qualify. Rejected — the explanation was triggered by David's insult, not by a deliberate curiosity-creation move, and the rep had no follow-up anchor to capitalize on the curiosity it produced.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep that the explanation worked — acknowledge the partial win at 01:45. Then show the shorter path: 'Actually that's a great question — the specialist will walk you through exactly how that works. Let me make sure you qualify first.' The goal is to redirect the curiosity toward the appointment, not to eliminate the explanation entirely. Speed and scope discipline, not suppression.

quote: I've never heard of such a thing.
stewart_read: This is the call's first genuine curiosity signal — David is surprised and engaged. The rep acknowledged it ('Yeah. No. It's super neat.') and immediately pivoted to orphaned-flow qualification. That pivot was procedurally correct but missed the opportunity to anchor the curiosity: 'That's exactly what our specialist will show you — let's get you on the calendar.' The moment was not wasted, but it was not leveraged.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this moment as a booking hook. When a skeptical customer says 'I've never heard of that,' the correct move is to tie the curiosity directly to the appointment: 'That's exactly what our specialist walks through — they'll show you the numbers for your specific setup.' The rep had the curiosity; they just didn't convert it.

quote: to qualify for our financing here, you need a credit score above 650. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: The rep stated the credit threshold as 650. The schema schema specifies 670. This is a recurring floor-wide discrepancy — also observed in SESSION18_fcbf33c1 — not a one-off error. Either the active threshold has changed and the schema is stale, or reps are consistently understating the requirement. Until Kenny resolves this, every setter call carries the risk of booking leads who clear 650 but fail the actual 670 pull.
coaching_implication: Do not coach this rep in isolation — this is a floor-wide calibration issue. Bring the discrepancy to Kenny: is the active threshold 650 or 670? Once resolved, push the correct number to all reps in the same session. Coaching one rep on a number that may be wrong floor-wide creates more confusion than it solves.

quote: Unfortunately, everything's online. That makes it a little easy.
stewart_read: David made a self-deprecating joke — 'unfortunately' signals mild irony about his own tech comfort, and 'that makes it a little easy' is a small warmth signal. The rep responded with a flat 'Yeah.' and said nothing further for 13 seconds while David navigated the site. This is a missed rapport micro-moment: David opened a conversational door and the rep didn't walk through it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep stayed quiet to let David focus on the navigation task and avoid breaking his concentration. Plausible, but the flat 'Yeah.' with no follow-up warmth suggests the rep simply didn't register the joke rather than deliberately holding space.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep to match the customer's energy when they offer it. A simple 'Ha — that's the best kind of easy' or 'Good, makes my job easier too' keeps the warmth alive without breaking David's focus. These micro-moments compound across a call; missing them repeatedly is why the call felt transactional throughout.

quote: 168 or something. Now the issue I have is where do I put this thing?
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-value moment. David confirms an existing Ion estimate (~$168) and surfaces a concrete, solvable objection — placement logistics. This is not a brush-off; it is a decision gate. The rep had one move: bridge the placement question to the specialist as the person who solves exactly this, and use that bridge to lock the appointment. Instead the rep admitted ignorance and offered a passive callback.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep deferred to Elijah deliberately because placement is a site-specific technical question that genuinely requires a specialist. Partially valid — the rep is right that this is closer-tier content. But the strategic move is to use that fact as the booking hook ('That's exactly why we get you on with Elijah — he'll assess your setup and tell you exactly where it goes'), not to admit ignorance and exit.
coaching_implication: This is the priority coaching moment. Walk the rep through the bridge move: 'That's exactly the kind of thing Elijah figures out on the call — he's done this dozens of times and will tell you exactly where it fits. Let's get you on his calendar this week.' The placement concern is not a disqualifier; it is the appointment reason. The rep needs to see that.

quote: Now the existing battery is inside the garage, but I'm kind of out of space inside the garage.
stewart_read: David elaborated on the placement concern with a specific detail — existing battery in the garage, space constraint. This is the second touch on the same concern (see multi_touch_patterns). The rep repeated the Elijah callback offer without adding any new framing, urgency, or information. Two touches on the same objection, same response both times — no escalation, no bridge, no booking.
coaching_implication: Pair this moment with 09:11 in the coaching session. Show the rep that David raised the same concern twice in 24 seconds — that repetition is a signal of genuine anxiety, not casual conversation. The rep's job at the second touch is to escalate the bridge: 'David, this is exactly why I want to get Elijah on the phone with you — he can look at your setup and tell you whether it goes on the exterior wall, the other side of the garage, or somewhere else entirely. What does your week look like?' Two touches with no escalation is a missed close.

quote: Okay. Yeah. Let me think about it. It'll probably be a couple of months or so.
stewart_read: David's soft deferral is the direct result of the rep's passive close at 09:54. The rep offered 'I could have Elijah call you' — a conditional, low-commitment framing — and David responded with a conditional, low-commitment exit. The rep then accepted it immediately with 'Just give us a call when you're ready.' No time anchor, no urgency, no locked callback. The call ends with no booking and no structured re-contact.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep recognized David was not ready to book and chose not to push, preserving goodwill for a future contact. Rejected — the rep had an existing Ion estimate on file (confirmed at 09:06) and a solvable objection. This was not a strategic retreat; it was an unforced surrender of a warm re-contact.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on the locked-callback distinction from the schema (soft_outcome_distinction directive): 'give us a call when you're ready' is not a callback — it is a graceful exit. The move here is: 'Totally understand — let me put a note in for Elijah to reach out in a couple months. Would early [month] work, or is later in the month better?' Even a soft time anchor converts a lost call into a structured re-contact. The rep needs to understand that 'we'll touch base' and a locked time are not the same outcome.

quote: Yeah. I already have an estimate from, call it, ICON or something like that.
stewart_read: David's confusion about the company name ('ICON or something') and the rep's confirmation that it is Ion ('This is ION Solar. Does that maybe ring a bell? Yeah. That's who I have an estimate for.') reveals that David already has an Ion estimate on file — a prior engagement the rep did not know about until this moment. The rep's response was to look it up in the system, which is correct procedure. However, the rep did not use the prior engagement as a booking hook: 'You already talked to us — great, that means Elijah already has your setup. Let's get you back on his calendar to go over the numbers.'
coaching_implication: Coach the rep to treat a confirmed prior Ion engagement as a warm re-contact signal, not just a data point. The prior estimate is a trust asset — David already vetted Ion once. The move is: 'Perfect — Elijah already has your design. Let's get you back on his calendar so he can walk you through the battery options for your specific system.' This converts the prior engagement from a neutral fact into a booking reason.
